hub_subtitle: Conversação para Viagens, Encontros Reais e Cultura

lesson.menu_title: Talking About Passion -- Cycling, Adventure and the Stories That Define You
lesson.menu_desc: Reading and telling adventure stories: gerunds and infinitives, and the words of the open road -- 28 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 4 -- Talking About Passion
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Marcelo de Azevedo Souza | Aula 4 -- Talking About Passion

listenings.text: Hi Marcelo, it's Fiona, from the hostel in Tirana. What a night. I still can't stop thinking about your story of riding through Yemen. Most people avoid talking about the hard parts, but you made the suffering sound almost beautiful. Listen, a few of us are planning an off-road trip through the Albanian Alps next spring, lots of steep climbs and rough terrain, exactly the kind of thing you love. I know it is demanding, but the views are worth every drop of sweat. I would really love for you to join us. Think about it. And remember what you said: you don't ride to arrive, you ride to feel alive. Take care, and keep pedaling.

Welcome back to the adventure show. My guest today has cycled across four continents, and I asked him the question everyone asks: why do you do it? His answer stayed with me. He said the thrill is not the point. The point is the solitude, the silence, and the strange freedom of being completely alone with the road. He told me that finishing a brutal climb is the most rewarding feeling he knows, harder than any success at work. And here is the part I love: he said adventure is not about being brave. It is about learning to pace yourself, to keep going when your body wants to stop, and to enjoy the journey instead of rushing to the end. That, he said, is a lesson for life, not just for cycling.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: giving a satisfying sense of achievement
a sudden strong feeling of excitement
away from paved roads, on rough ground
the peaceful state of being alone
making you feel very excited and alive
the ability to keep going through effort or pain
a longer road chosen for its beautiful views
needing a lot of effort and skill

paras: For Marcelo, cycling has never been about the exercise. It is about the feeling. He loves riding through places most people never see, and he tends to avoid the easy, paved roads. Give him rough terrain, a steep climb and an off-road trail, and he is happy. 'I ride to feel alive,' he says. 'The solitude is the whole point.'

The hard trips are the ones he remembers. Cycling through Albania, Morocco and Yemen was demanding, sometimes even brutal, but that is exactly why it was so rewarding. He remembers deciding to keep going up a mountain when his body wanted to stop. Reaching the top was the most exhilarating moment of his life. 'You learn to pace yourself,' he explains. 'You stop rushing to arrive, and you start enjoying the ride.'

It is hard to put the feeling into words, and he knows it. When people ask why he does it, he just smiles. 'You had to be there,' he says. Adventure, for Marcelo, is not about being brave. It is about choosing the scenic route, again and again, and never trading the journey for a shortcut.
reading.source: Adapted from an adventure-cycling feature, The Long Way Round

reading.prompt: What is the main idea of the text?
choices: Marcelo cycles mainly to stay fit and win races.
For Marcelo, cycling is about the feeling, the solitude and the challenge, not the exercise or the destination.
Marcelo only enjoys easy, paved roads with beautiful views.

items: Marcelo prefers easy, paved roads to rough, off-road trails.
The text says he tends to avoid the easy, paved roads and loves rough terrain and steep climbs.
The hard, demanding trips are the ones he remembers most.
The second paragraph says the hard trips are the ones he remembers, and that is why they were so rewarding.
Marcelo finds it easy to explain the feeling to other people.
The text says it is hard to put the feeling into words -- he just says 'You had to be there.'
For Marcelo, adventure is about the journey, not a shortcut.
The last paragraph says he chooses the scenic route and never trades the journey for a shortcut.

parts: through wild places, and he tends
to keep going up a mountain when his body wanted
the ride instead of rushing to arrive.

gapfill.text: Notice: some verbs are followed by the -ing form (a gerund) -- 'love riding', 'enjoy riding', 'remember deciding'. Other verbs are followed by 'to' + the base verb (an infinitive) -- 'tend to avoid', 'want to stop', 'learn to pace'. The verb before the gap tells you which form to use.

bank.label: Useful language for talking about your passion
items: What I love most about ... is ...
I find it hard to explain ...
The thing that gets me is ...
It's not just about ... -- it's about ...
I wouldn't trade it for anything.

lf.text: Notice: after 'love' and 'enjoy' we use the -ing form (riding, being). After 'tend' and 'find it hard' we use 'to' + the base verb (to avoid, to explain). The verb before decides the form. Some verbs, like 'like' and 'prefer', accept both with almost the same meaning.

items: Tell the story of one cycling trip that changed you: where you went, what was demanding, and how it felt to keep going. Use gerunds and infinitives.
Explain why you love adventure cycling to someone who has never done it. Use 'What I love most is ...' and 'It's not just about ... -- it's about ...'
Describe a moment that was hard to put into words: a steep climb, a view, the solitude. Try 'You had to be there' and 'I wouldn't trade it for anything.'
Talk about what you avoid and what you look for on a ride: off-road trails, the scenic route, rough terrain. Use 'I tend to avoid ...' and 'I love ...'

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = riding, 2 = to avoid, 3 = deciding, 4 = to stop, 5 = to pace, 6 = riding.
Model (gerunds): 'I love riding off-road, and I enjoy being alone on a long, demanding climb.'
Model (infinitives): 'I tend to avoid the easy roads, and I have learned to pace myself and to keep going.'
Gerund vs infinitive: after love / enjoy / avoid / keep use the -ing form; after tend / want / decide / learn / find it hard use 'to' + the base verb.
answerkey.note: Some verbs take both with the same meaning (like, prefer, start). A few change meaning: 'I stopped riding' (I ended the ride) vs 'I stopped to ride' (I paused in order to ride).
